title: Student loans: subsidized, unsubsidized, repayment plans
deck: Federal student loans use the same amortization formula as auto loans, with two twists: interest can accrue during school, and federal programs offer multiple repayment plans with different monthly payments.
objectives: Compare subsidized and unsubsidized federal student loans, analyze the effect of interest capitalization on the monthly payment, and identify the standard repayment options.

content.text: Imagine two students who each take out a $10,000 federal student loan at 6.5% to help cover college. They sign the paperwork on the same day and graduate four years later, on the same day. When they enter repayment, however, their loan balances are different. One owes $10,000; the other owes $12,600. The numbers are correct, and the difference, more than two grand on the same headline loan, is entirely the result of one checkbox on the student-aid application.

Federal student loans come in two flavors, subsidized and unsubsidized, and the distinction is exactly the kind of fine-print detail that matters enormously over time. This section explains the difference, walks through what happens to the principal during the in-school years, and applies the amortization formula from Section 4.2 to both flavors at repayment time. It also notes the standard repayment plans the federal program offers when graduation arrives.

A subsidized loan has its interest paid by the federal government during in-school years (and during certain deferment periods). The balance entering repayment is exactly the original principal, with no interest tacked on. An unsubsidized loan, by contrast, accrues interest from the moment it is disbursed. The student is not required to make payments while in school, but the unpaid interest piles up and is eventually capitalized, added to the principal, when repayment begins.

content.caption: Figure 4.5. Same $10,000 loan, same 6.5% rate, four years in school. The subsidized loan enters repayment at $10,000; the unsubsidized loan enters repayment at $12,600 because of capitalized interest.

content.text: Definition 4.5. A subsidized federal student loan is one for which the government pays the interest during in-school years and certain deferment periods. An unsubsidized federal student loan accrues interest from the day of disbursement; that interest is capitalized (added to the principal) at the start of repayment, increasing the principal P that enters the amortization formula.

Margin noteEligibility for subsidized loans is based on demonstrated financial need; unsubsidized loans are available to most students regardless of income. The numerical interest rate on both products is set by federal statute and is the same for both flavors in any given year. The only difference is who pays the in-school interest.

When repayment begins (typically six months after graduation, the “grace period”), federal borrowers choose a repayment plan. The standard 10-year plan uses the amortization formula at t = 10 years and produces the largest monthly payment but the lowest total cost. Extended plans run up to 25 years and lower the monthly payment at the cost of more total interest. Income-driven plans cap the monthly payment at a percentage of discretionary income and can include partial loan forgiveness after a long period of qualifying payments.

Common mistakeAssuming an unsubsidized loan is “the same loan, just bigger.” The unsubsidized loan is in fact a larger principal at the same rate, which means the monthly payment is also larger and the total interest paid is larger still. Capitalization moves money from the government's column to the borrower's, with no other change to the loan's terms.
A subsidized loan and an unsubsidized loan with the same number on the cover are not the same loan.

Example 4.5. Subsidized vs. unsubsidized: $10,000, 6.5%, 10-year payoff.

Two students each borrow $10,000 at 6.5% annual interest. Student A's loan is subsidized; Student B's is unsubsidized. Both spend four years in school, and both enter the standard 10-year repayment plan upon graduation. We want to compare the principal entering repayment, the monthly payment, and the total interest paid for each student.

Solution.

Step 1. Compute the principal at repayment for each student. Student A (subsidized): the government covers in-school interest, so PA = $10,000. Student B (unsubsidized): interest accrues at simple-interest rate I = Prt = 10,000 × 0.065 × 4 = $2,600, and that amount is capitalized, so PB = $10,000 + $2,600 = $12,600.

Step 2. Apply the amortization formula at 10 years. With r/12 ≈ 0.005417 and 12t = 120, the denominator is 1 − (1.005417)−120 ≈ 0.4768. So

M_A = 10,000 · [0.005417] / [0.4768] ≈ $113.55
M_B = 12,600 · [0.005417] / [0.4768] ≈ $143.07

Step 3. Compute total paid and total interest for each. Total payments: M × 120.

Student A: total ≈ 113.55 × 120 ≈ $13,626 , interest ≈ $3,626
Student B: total ≈ 143.07 × 120 ≈ $17,168 , interest ≈ $4,568 + $2,600 = $7,168

Student B pays nearly $30 more per month and almost twice as much total interest, on the same $10,000 of original borrowing. The single difference is whether the in-school interest was paid by the federal government or capitalized onto the principal at graduation.

In the wildFederal student loan disclosures and the National Student Loan Data System (NSLDS) show every borrower their current principal, the interest rate, and any capitalized interest on each loan. The standard 10-year repayment plan is the default; income-driven plans must be elected and renewed annually. Private student loans (from banks rather than the federal government) follow the same amortization math but typically lack the income-driven and forgiveness options.

1. A student borrows $8,000 of unsubsidized federal loans at 5% per year, spends 4 years in school, and lets the interest accrue. Using simple interest, how much interest is capitalized at the start of repayment?

    Show answer

    Answer. $1,600. The capitalized interest is I = Prt = 8,000 × 0.05 × 4 = 1,600.

  2. For the loan in the previous problem, what is the principal at the start of repayment?

    Show answer

    Answer. $9,600. The capitalized principal is 8,000 + 1,600 = 9,600.

  3. Using the standard 10-year amortization at 5% APR, compute the monthly payment on the $9,600 capitalized balance. Round to the nearest cent.

    Show answer

    Answer. About $101.81. With r/12 ≈ 0.004167 and 12t = 120, the denominator is 1 − (1.004167)−120 ≈ 0.3929, and M = 9,600 × 0.004167 / 0.3929 ≈ 101.81.

  4. Which of the following describes the difference between subsidized and unsubsidized federal student loans?

    1. Subsidized loans have a lower interest rate than unsubsidized loans.
    2. Subsidized loans have a longer repayment term than unsubsidized loans.
    3. The federal government pays in-school interest on subsidized loans but not on unsubsidized loans.
    4. Unsubsidized loans cannot be used for tuition.
    Show answer

    Answer. C. The rate, term, and uses are the same for both; the only difference is who pays the in-school interest.

  5. Which standard federal repayment plan produces the largest total interest paid over the life of the loan, all else equal?

    1. The 10-year standard plan
    2. The 25-year extended plan
    3. An income-driven plan with very low payments
    4. Both B and C, depending on the borrower's income.
    Show answer

    Answer. D. Longer repayment horizons and lower payments both stretch the balance across more periods, raising the total interest. Income-driven plans can in some cases include forgiveness, which partially offsets the higher interest.

  6. If two students borrow the same amount at the same rate, but Student A's loan is subsidized and Student B's is unsubsidized, then at the start of repayment…

    1. Student A and Student B have identical balances.
    2. Student A has a smaller balance than Student B.
    3. Student B has a smaller balance than Student A.
    4. Cannot tell without knowing each student's GPA.
    Show answer

    Answer. B. Capitalization adds the accrued in-school interest to Student B's principal, leaving Student A with the smaller balance.

Student loans showed how capitalization quietly changes the principal at repayment. Mortgages reveal an even subtler effect: how each monthly payment splits between interest and principal, and how that split shifts month by month over a thirty-year term. Section 4.6 closes the chapter with the longest and largest amortizing loan most people will ever sign, and a careful look at why the early years of a mortgage feel like they are barely paying anything down.
